t: — most frequent; hand washing, disinfection compulsions.
The patient accepts that obsessions are their own thoughts, but unwanted, irrational, or unacceptable (insight varies — DSM-5-TR insight qualifiers: good / poor / absent).
Presence of obsession, compulsion, or both.
repeated, persistent, unwanted thoughts/images/impulses; creates distress; patient attempts to suppress or neutralize with other thoughts/actions.

Repetitive behavior (hand washing, checking, ordering) or mental act (counting, praying) performed as a response to an obsession or according to rigid rules; aimed at reducing anxiety, but either unrealistic or excessive.
Obsession/compulsions take >1 hour per day or cause significant distress/functional impairment.
Exclusion of substance or medical condition.
Not better explained by another mental disorder.
DSM-5-TR — insight specifiers (good/fair — poor — absent/delusional); tic-related subtype.
ICD-11 — parallel; hoarding (6B24), BDD (6B21), olfactory reference (6B22) separate categories.
NICE CG31 (2005, 2019 surveillance) — diagnostics via clinical interview + Y-BOCS.
SCID-5, Y-BOCS (Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale) — the gold standard severity scale; CY-BOCS for children.
Comorbidity (depression, anxiety, tic, autism, BDD, hoarding).
Persistent personality pattern, ego-syntonic; OCPD is ego-dystonic.
Insight at the delusion level; insight in OCD is variable.
First-line psychotherapy: ERP (Exposure and Response Prevention)
— patient is exposed to feared object/situation and does not perform compulsion. 12–20 sessions. Evidence: Foa E.B. et al. RCTs; effect substantial.
First-line pharmacotherapy: SSRIs at high dose
– fluoxetine (40–80 mg), sertraline (200 mg), paroxetine (40–60 mg), fluvoxamine (200–300 mg), escitalopram (20–40 mg). In OCD standard dose higher than for depression
— superior to monotherapy in moderate-to-severe OCD (Foa E.B. et al. Am J Psychiatry 2005).
— First-line alternative; in case of non-response to SSRI; anticholinergic and cardiac side effects.
Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS — ventral capsule/ventral striatum), neurosurgery (capsulotomy, cingulotomy); EKT ineffective; ketamine/esketamine rapid effect studies ongoing
— long-term; ≥ 1–2 years after remission, lifelong in case of multiple relapses.
NICE CG31 (2005, 2019 surveillance) — stepped approach by severity; ERP first-line; SSRIs for moderate-severe.
APA Practice Guideline for OCD (2007) — same; clomipramine strong based on evidence, but second-line due to side effects.
WFSBP (Bandelow B. et al. 2008) — SSRI dose ranges and titration
NICE CG31 PANDAS — routine antibiotic prophylaxis not recommended.
Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP) — Meyer (Meyer V.), Foa (Foa E.B.)

The patient is exposed to feared object (e.g., "dirty" object) and does not perform compulsion (washing hands); anxiety gradually decreases (habituation). Standard course 12–20 sessions, 1–2 times per week; more intensive format (daily) is effective. Evidence: Foa E.B. et al. Am J Psychiatry 2005.
Addition to ERP; re-evaluation of thoughts about obsession; targets "thought-action fusion" (TAF) and hyper-responsibility cognitions.
Yale-Brown Obsessive-Compulsive Scale (Y-BOCS — Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale) — Goodman (Goodman W.K.)
Criteria: 10 items; 5 obsessions + 5 compulsions; gold standard; CY-BOCS in children.
— In OCD significantly higher dose than standard depression dose is required; titration over 8–12 weeks; in case of non-response dose is increased to maximum.
— Evidence in OCD strong (CMI multicenter trial 1991); cardiac and anticholinergic side effects; second-line.
Surgical intervention for refractory severe OCD; target ventral capsule/ventral striatum. Greenberg B.D. et al. Mol Psychiatry 2010.
Chronic course, but significant improvement with adequate treatment.
Komorbid depressiya, tikli, autizm pis proqnoz markeri.
Early onset and poor insight — more difficult treatment.
Myth 1: "OCPD is just a personality trait related to cleanliness and orderliness"

Clinical OCD is ego-dystonic, causes distress and functional impairment; obsessive-compulsive personality disorder (OCPD) is ego-syntonic distinct disorder. Clinical diagnosis based on Y-BOCS severity and duration.
OCD is dysregulation of CSTC circuit; not voluntarily controlled; ERP and SSRIs affect it at a neurobiological level.
Myth 3: "SSRIs work in OCD at depression doses"
In OCD, SSRIs require high doses (fluoxetine 60–80 mg, sertralin 200 mg); depression dose (e.g., fluoxetine 20 mg) often does not respond.
Myth 4: "ERP causes trauma because it keeps the patient in a feared situation"
ERP is structured, stepwise, and conducted with patient consent; demonstrates long-term proven efficacy.
Myth 5: "PANDAS is the main cause of OCD, everyone should take antibiotics"
NIMH 2017 — PANDAS is a rare event; routine antistreptolysin O tests and antibiotic prophylaxis are not recommended; only if a classic clinical picture is present.
Myth 6: "Allowing compulsions to be performed calms symptoms"
Permission (accommodation) for compulsions strengthens OCD; reducing family accommodation is a crucial treatment component.
Myth 7: "Herbal preparations (St John's Wort, kava, omega-3) cure OCD"
none have proven efficacy for OCD; St John's Wort carries risk of interaction with SSRIs.
NICE CG31. Obsessive-compulsive disorder and body dysmorphic disorder. 2005, 2019 surveillance.
